meta.title: Lev Meta-Substrate Pattern
meta.description: Declaration to compiler to IR to runtime across FlowMind, Poly, core modules, plugins, and specs.

props.title: Declaration -> Compiler -> IR -> Runtime
props.subtitle: A recurring Lev pattern, but not a universal religion.

props.label: PATTERN
props.title: The shape that keeps reappearing
props.subtitle: The strongest subsystems share a four-layer structure, with an optional fifth layer for discovery or federation.

props.content:
The pattern is simplest in plain English: authored truth becomes normalized meaning, then a stable intermediate form, then a runtime or projection layer consumes that stable form. FlowMind, Poly, orchestration, graph, and UI all already lean this way.

The useful part is not that every module must have a named compiler package. The useful part is that serious subsystems stop being mushy when they can answer four questions honestly: what is the declaration, where is the lowering seam, what is the stable form, and who owns runtime.

props.caption: The reusable shape
props.content:
flowchart TD
  A[Declaration\n.flow.yaml / config / DSL / schema] --> B[Compiler / Lowering / Normalization]
  B --> C[Stable Form / IR\nGraphDocument / PolyIR / LevUI IR / typed result]
  C --> D[Runtime / Projection / Execution\nexec / CLI / Stack / renderer / adapter]
  D --> E[Optional Discovery / Federation / Composition]

props.label: FIT CLASSES
props.title: Where the pattern is strong, partial, or forced
props.subtitle: The sweep only becomes useful if we name the exceptions.

props.title: ✅ Strong fit
props.content:
FlowMind, Orchestration, Poly, Graph, UI, Prompt-Stack, Validator, Timetravel, Browser Cascade, LevUI.

These own a real declaration surface, a real lowering seam, and a stable form that multiple consumers can reuse.

props.title: 🟨 Partial fit
props.content:
Memory, Config, Build, EventBus, Storage, Telemetry, Defendr, System Dashboard, Code Graph.

These usually have normalization and stable envelopes, but not always a first-class named IR or full compiler stage.

props.title: ⛔ Non-fit
props.content:
Domain, Logger, thin wrappers like Graph Adapters, and some runtime-only helpers.

Do not invent fake compilers or fake IRs just to satisfy the pattern. Some modules are contracts or utilities and should stay that way.

props.label: SURVEY
props.title: What the repo sweep actually found
props.subtitle: Core, plugins, and specs all converged on the same broad conclusion.

rows.note: This is the architectural backbone where the doctrine is already real.

rows.examples: Prompt-Stack, Validator, Timetravel, Browser Cascade, Lev Now
rows.note: Plugins fit best when they own either a small DSL or a stable typed result form.

rows.examples: FlowMind and UI explicit; Poly/Forge partial; memory/event/storage weak
rows.note: The doctrine exists more clearly in design than in some runtime specs.
These are not missing architecture. They are different kinds of modules.

props.title: 📌 Key read
props.content: The pattern is strong enough to guide scaffolding and generated software, but only if we preserve fit classes. The win is repeatable shape, not total uniformity.

props.label: SCAFFOLDING
props.title: How to apply it going forward
props.subtitle: This is the actionable doctrine for plugin scaffolding and dynamic generation.

props.content:
1. What is the declaration surface?
2. Where is the lowering or normalization seam?
3. What is the stable intermediate form?
4. Who owns runtime or projection?
5. Is there a discovery / federation layer above runtime?

If you cannot answer those honestly, the subsystem is probably still mushy.
If the answers are fake, do not force the pattern.

props.title: ⚡ Immediate impact
props.content: For the current workstream, nothing changes about the next execution step: implement Poly protocol nouns, the Poly compiler seam, fixture goldens, and the CLI proof. This note is a doctrine lens, not a reason to reopen the Poly architecture.
